2025 · NeurIPS 2025 · Agents

WebThinker: Empowering Large Reasoning Models with Deep Research Capability

Take an open 32B reasoning model, and instead of bolting a fixed retrieval pipeline onto it, let the model itself drive the research: search the web, click deeper into pages, and draft a report — all inside its own chain of thought, then sharpen the habit with iterative online DPO.

♪ listen — audio episode
3-voice deep dive · mp3 ↗ · rss feed ↗
Xiaoxi Li, Jiajie Jin, Guanting Dong, Hongjin Qian, Yongkang Wu, Ji-Rong Wen, Yutao Zhu, Zhicheng Dou · Renmin University of China / BAAI / Huawei Poisson Lab
venue NeurIPS 2025 citations 299 topic Agents source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-06-21
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Large reasoning models (LRMs) — OpenAI-o1, DeepSeek-R1, Qwen's QwQ — are very good at long chains of thought, but that reasoning runs entirely on static internal knowledge. Ask one a question whose answer lives on the open web, or ask it to write a comprehensive research report synthesising many sources, and the same model that aces math olympiads stalls: it cannot look anything up, and it has no mechanism for assembling evidence it has never seen.

The obvious fix — bolt retrieval onto the model — is where existing open-source "deep search" agents get stuck. They wrap the LRM in a predefined RAG workflow: retrieve for the query (standard RAG), or decompose the query, retrieve for each sub-query, and merge (query-planning / iterative RAG). These pipelines are rigid in three ways the paper highlights (Figure 1, below): the search is shallow — usually one round of top-k snippets with no ability to click a link and read further; reasoning and retrieval are decoupled — the model reasons, then retrieves, rather than searching the moment it hits a knowledge gap; and report writing happens all at once at the end, after retrieval is frozen, so the draft can't request the specific evidence a half-written section turns out to need. The result, in the authors' words, is a "lack of thinking depth & coherence."

Meanwhile the systems that actually work well — OpenAI Deep Research, Grok3 DeeperSearch, Gemini Deep Research — are proprietary. WebThinker's goal is a universal, open, end-to-end framework where the reasoning model is the orchestrator, not a component slotted into a hand-built pipeline.

// how it works

The method

WebThinker promotes the LRM from "thing that gets called" to "thing that does the calling." During its normal reasoning, the model can invoke tools from an available set T; the generation factorises into reasoning-with-tools (each step conditioned on the outputs of every tool call made so far) followed by final-output generation. Concretely it runs in two modes.

1 · Problem-Solving Mode — the Deep Web Explorer

The core tool is the Deep Web Explorer. When the main reasoning chain hits a knowledge gap, it spins up the explorer — itself an LRM-driven sub-agent — with a query. The explorer has two primitive actions: a search engine (issue a query, get web pages) and a navigation tool (click links or buttons to move deeper into the pages it's looking at). Critically, it runs its own reasoning loop to decide, after each result, whether to search again or click deeper, recursing until it has what it needs. It then returns a single concise distilled output back to the main chain — not a dump of raw pages. This hierarchical delegation is what turns "shallow search" into genuine multi-hop web exploration: the main reasoner stays focused on the problem while the explorer does the legwork.

2 · Report Generation Mode — Autonomous Think-Search-and-Draft

For open-ended report tasks, WebThinker interleaves reasoning, searching (via the explorer), and writing in real time. Every page the explorer reads is accumulated in a document memory M. The model wields three report tools — draft a section, check the current report, edit the report — each executed by a separate assistant LLM so that high-level orchestration (the main LRM) is cleanly split from the detailed text manipulation. When the main model decides to write, it emits an editing instruction e; the assistant fetches the top-k relevant documents from M and produces the updated report content grounded in them. Because drafting happens as evidence arrives, a half-finished section can trigger exactly the search it needs next, instead of the model writing everything in one shot after retrieval has stopped.

3 · Reinforcement learning via iterative online DPO

A capable backbone doesn't automatically use these tools well, so WebThinker is fine-tuned with on-policy RL. For each query drawn from complex datasets (SuperGPQA, WebWalkerQA, OpenThoughts, NaturalReasoning, NuminaMath, Glaive), the model self-samples n trajectories, and preference pairs (preferred Rw, dispreferred Rl) are constructed by three rules applied in priority order: (1) correctness / report quality first (a correct answer always beats an incorrect one); (2) tool efficiency — among correct trajectories, fewer total tool calls wins; (3) thinking conciseness — among equally-correct, equal-tool trajectories, the shorter one wins when the length ratio exceeds a threshold γ. The model is trained on these pairs with the standard DPO objective:

LDPO = − E(Rw,Rl)∼D log σ ( β log πθ(Rw)πref(Rw) − β log πθ(Rl)πref(Rl) ) // raise the likelihood of preferred trajectories over dispreferred ones, relative to a frozen reference policy (conditioning on I, q omitted)

The "iterative online" part matters: after training, the updated policy re-samples fresh trajectories, new preference pairs are built from those, the reference policy is reset to the current model (πref ← πθ), and the loop repeats. The main results use 2 iterations. The backbone is QwQ-32B; the assistant uses Qwen2.5-Instruct at the same size; search is Bing Web Search (US-EN, k=10) with page content fetched via Crawl4AI; generation runs up to 81,920 tokens.

// what they found

The results

On four knowledge-intensive reasoning benchmarks — GPQA (PhD-level science), GAIA (general AI-assistant tasks), WebWalkerQA (deep web navigation), and Humanity's Last Exam (HLE) — WebThinker dominates other 32B systems on Pass@1. The training-free WebThinker-32B-Base already beats Search-o1 everywhere (e.g. +22.9% on WebWalkerQA, +20.4% on HLE), and WebThinker-32B-RL pushes further to the best 32B numbers across the board. The single largest jumps are on the tasks that most demand live retrieval: GAIA climbs from QwQ's 22.3 → 48.5 and WebWalkerQA from 4.3 → 46.5. RL beats Search-o1 on GAIA by +21.9% relative, and on HLE its 15.8 even edges out o3-mini (High) at 14.0.

Here are the 32B Pass@1 averages, with larger/closed-source systems shown for reference (greyed in the paper):

Method (32B unless noted)GPQAGAIAWebWalkerHLE
QwQ-32B (direct, no retrieval)64.122.34.39.6
RAG-QwQ-32B64.632.031.27.2
Search-o1-32B67.239.834.110.8
WebThinker-32B-Base68.744.741.913.0
WebThinker-32B-RL70.748.546.515.8
DeepSeek-R1-671B (ref)74.231.110.08.6
o1-preview (ref)73.3—9.9—
OpenAI Deep Research (ref)—67.4—26.6

Report generation. On the Glaive open-ended research tasks, scored 1–10 on completeness, thoroughness, factuality, and coherence (averaged across DeepSeek-R1 and GPT-4o judges), WebThinker-32B-RL reaches an overall 8.1 (Comp 8.3, Thorough 8.4, Fact 7.7, Coh 7.9), with the Base at 8.0 — ahead of Gemini2.0 Deep Research (7.9), Grok3 DeeperSearch (6.5), and the RAG baselines (RAG-DeepSeek-R1 6.8, RAG-Qwen2.5-72B 5.9). A t-SNE of report embeddings shows WebThinker's reports spreading into broader sub-clusters per topic — the authors read this as wider, more diverse information coverage.

It generalises across backbones. Cold-start SFT on 7.8k QwQ-32B trajectories followed by RL lets WebThinker ride on DeepSeek-R1 at 7B, 14B, and 32B — and it beats direct generation and standard RAG at every size. The 7B gains are enormous in relative terms: +174.4% on GAIA and +422.6% on WebWalkerQA over direct generation (and +82.9% / +161.3% over standard RAG).

Ablations name the load-bearing part. Removing the Deep Web Explorer is by far the biggest hit — complex-reasoning average collapses from 45.4 → 38.3 (and report quality from 8.1 → 7.7); even disabling just link-clicking drops the average to 42.6. On reports, removing autonomous drafting causes the largest fall (8.1 → 6.6). RL itself is the smaller, second-order gain: online DPO (45.4) over offline DPO (43.2) over the untrained Base (42.1).

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The open-source-vs-open-source story is solid: 32B-vs-32B, WebThinker clearly beats QwQ, RAG variants, and Search-o1, and the ablations make a convincing case that the Deep Web Explorer — not the RL — is the engine. The trouble is in how far the headline claim ("outperforms strong proprietary systems") is stretched, and in what a live-web agent leaves unmeasured.

The "beats Gemini / Grok Deep Research" result is entirely on report generation, where the grader is an LLM-as-judge (DeepSeek-R1 + GPT-4o) scoring a subjective 1–10 rubric with no reported human validation and a compressed score band (5.9–8.1) — and one of the judges, GPT-4o, is itself among the systems being compared, a textbook self-preference confound. On the verifiable benchmarks, where the numbers are hard, the 32B model still trails the closed Deep Research systems by a wide margin: GAIA 48.5 vs OpenAI Deep Research's 67.4, HLE 15.8 vs 26.6 — and on GPQA it never passes DeepSeek-R1-671B (74.2) or o1-preview (73.3). The "rivals proprietary" framing only holds on the soft, judged task.

The deeper structural worry is search-time contamination. WebThinker is, by design, querying the live internet at test time on public benchmarks (GPQA, GAIA, WebWalkerQA, HLE) — so it can retrieve the benchmark items themselves, leaderboard write-ups, or solution discussions, an advantage a closed-book model structurally cannot have and a confound the paper does not audit or filter for. Accuracy is also adjudicated by Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct rather than exact match, adding grader leniency on top.

baselines

Fair within the open 32B tier (QwQ, RAG, Search-o1). But the proprietary win is report-only and LLM-judged; on objective tasks WebThinker is well behind OpenAI Deep Research, and several head-to-head cells (OpenAI DR on GPQA/WebWalker) are simply blank.

contamination

Untouched, and it's the central risk here: a live-web agent can surface the exact public benchmark questions and their answers. No page filtering, freshness control, or contamination audit is reported.

what's missed

Cost. A recursive LRM-driven explorer with an 81,920-token budget makes many search/click calls, yet there's no report of tokens, latency, API calls, or $ per task — so the gains over Search-o1 aren't on a compute axis. Pass@1 only (no pass@k or variance at temperature 0.7), and report quality rests on a single dataset (Glaive) with no human raters.

would raise confidence

Human evaluation of reports (plus judge-agreement / self-preference checks); a contamination filter or freshly-authored, non-public questions; accuracy-vs-cost curves against Search-o1 and the closed systems; pass@k / error bars; and filling in the missing proprietary cells for apples-to-apples comparison.

// bottom line

A clean, genuinely open recipe for turning a 32B reasoning model into a deep-research agent — and a clear demonstration that letting the model drive search, navigation, and drafting beats rigid RAG pipelines and Search-o1, with depth of web exploration (not RL) as the load-bearing piece. But the "beats proprietary Deep Research" headline rests on an unvalidated LLM-judge report score; on the verifiable benchmarks it stays well behind OpenAI/Google, and the live-web setup leaves contamination and per-task cost essentially unaudited.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Let the reasoning model drive. Replacing a predefined RAG workflow with a model that autonomously searches, clicks deeper, and drafts inside its own chain of thought lifts an open QwQ-32B from 22.3 → 48.5 on GAIA and 4.3 → 46.5 on WebWalkerQA. Ablating the Deep Web Explorer is the single biggest drop (45.4 → 38.3 avg) — depth of exploration is the core win.
  2. RL is the polish, not the engine. Iterative online DPO over correctness → tool-efficiency → conciseness preferences adds only ~3 points (Base 42.1 → RL 45.4 avg) and barely moves report quality. The capability comes from the agent architecture; the RL refines how cleanly it's used.
  3. Open can match closed on judged reports, but not yet on verifiable tasks. WebThinker-32B tops Gemini/Grok Deep Research on LLM-scored report generation (8.1 avg) yet still trails OpenAI Deep Research on GAIA (48.5 vs 67.4) and HLE (15.8 vs 26.6) — and because it searches the live web on public benchmarks, contamination and per-task cost remain open questions.
